excerpt_en: The Mediterranean trip ended and I returned to Ireland, arriving at 1 a.m. and spending the night at the airport. I had a 5:30 a.m. meeting for a project in Hiroshima, then took a 6:40 bus to pick up the keys for my new place. I accidentally took the wrong direction and lost time in freezing 0°C weather — about 20°C colder than Cyprus. The house is in the city but not central, about 30 minutes by tram, and the room is spacious. After a short nap, I went to the Japanese-owned salon “MARU,” where I also placed FOMUS masu at their second location. Even on the day I returned, I worked. The next two months in Ireland will be about pushing the FOMUS brand hard.
body_en:
The Mediterranean expedition is over,

and I’m back in Ireland.

I arrived at 1 a.m.,

so I spent time at the airport.

At 5:30 a.m. I had a meeting

for a project in Japan,

mainly for a plan we’re moving forward in Hiroshima.

Then at 6:40 a.m. I got on a bus

to pick up the keys to the house I’ll live in from today.

And here my clumsiness kicked in.

Apparently I got on the wrong direction,

so instead of heading south of the airport,

I went north, wasting a lot of time.

And the temperature was 0°C.

The difference from Cyprus was about 20°C,

so it felt brutally cold.

After all that,

I got the keys.

The house I’ll live in is in the city,

but not the center,

about 30 minutes by tram.

It’s not an area where tourists are around.

It was 9 a.m., and another person lives in my room,

but they weren’t there.

The room is very spacious and comfortable.

I had already viewed it online,

so there weren’t any surprises.

I sorted my luggage lightly, took a shower, and napped.

I hadn’t slept at all,

so I slept for about three hours,

then went to the Japanese-owned hair salon “MARU.”

I had booked it before the Mediterranean trip.

The last time I cut my hair

was before the Medoc Marathon,

so it had been about three months.

For me, salons are also places to gather information,

so I heard a lot of stories.

And my hair turned out great!

Also, “MARU” has two locations, and

I had placed FOMUS masu at the one I visited before,

and this time I placed FOMUS masu at their other shop too!

Even on the day I returned to Ireland,

I was working properly. lol

Since the masu are placed at reception,

everyone who comes to the salon has to pay at the counter,

so they’ll pass by them there.

In the future, I want to be able to sell FOMUS masu here too,

so for now I want to build as many achievements as possible in Ireland.

If they say, “We want to place FOMUS masu,”

that would be the strongest possible result.

I’ll work hard to make that happen.

And with that,

even after coming back to Ireland,

it was a busy day.

The house is set and paid for,

so over these next two months,

I want to spend my time in Ireland

seriously expanding the FOMUS brand!

excerpt_en: It was Wednesday and I had no plans until a Japanese meetup in the evening, so I focused on final preparations for the Japanese Embassy event in Ireland the next day. I organized an English presentation on FOMUS and explained what masu are. I brought about 120 masu (coated and plain), enough for the 30-person event. At 7 p.m. I joined a Japanese meetup at Yamamori, met more people, and learned that it’s important to talk to all kinds of ages. After the event I went to Taiki’s house to pick up masu shipped from Japan, packed them into my suitcase, tote, and backpack, and headed home. Tomorrow is the big event.
body_en:
It’s Wednesday,

so I don’t have anything planned

until the Japanese meetup event at night.

So,

I did final adjustments for tomorrow’s event

at the Japanese Embassy in Ireland,

thinking about how to let people know about FOMUS.

I don’t even know if I’ll use it, but

I organized FOMUS presentation materials,

made everything in English,

and made it easy for anyone to understand,

including a section on “What is a masu?”

I’ve served sake with masu at events many times,

so I’m not worried about that,

but how I approach people

depends on the atmosphere,

so no matter how much I prepare,

I don’t know if it will be useful.

That’s why I’ll prepare as much as possible.

For tomorrow’s event,

I brought about 120 masu,

both coated and uncoated.

The embassy event is said to be around 30 people,

so even if all the coated masu are used, it should be enough.

Anyway, I’ll prepare as much as I can.

Everything else is up to luck.

Let’s see how it goes. lol

🔻To a Japanese meetup

From 7 p.m., I went to Yamamori

and joined a Japanese meetup.

At first, I didn’t know many people,

but gradually more showed up.

There was a Japanese girl there,

and she came on exchange,

and said she’ll be staying a bit longer.

I wanted to visit a university too,

so she said I can come hang out sometime! lol

There’s a lot to learn from older people, business owners,

but there are also things you can learn and opportunities you get from younger university students,

so I felt again that it’s important to talk to all kinds of people.

After the event,

I went to Taiki’s house, where I had stayed two weeks earlier.

The reason was that I shipped masu from Japan to Ireland,

but at the time I didn’t have a house,

so I had them delivered to his workplace.

So I went to pick them up.

The masu I mentioned in the first half arrived safely.

I packed them into the suitcase I bought in Greece,

my usual tote bag, and my backpack,

stuffed them full, and went home.

Tomorrow is the big event.

I’m going all in!!

excerpt_en: At night I went to an event at the Japanese Embassy in Ireland. About a month ago I joined a Japan–Ireland Chamber of Commerce event, connected with embassy staff, and after a visit to the embassy, they asked me to provide masu for this event. I arrived early to set up and then there was a one-hour Irish talk show. After that, my main part began: promoting masu with Japanese sake. Many Irish and Japanese guests enjoyed drinking from masu, and some even considered purchasing. I talked with the ambassador and other officials, and even heard they want me again at year-end. After the event, we drank sake and red wine together and chatted until after 9 p.m. This opportunity came from meeting people and moving constantly over the past months. The ambassador even said I could use his name when pitching. From here I’ll approach Irish companies, and I’m also moving forward with a new gift brand, “MASUKAME,” focused on cute designs beyond the FOMUS logo.
body_en:
So,

at night I went to an event at the Japanese Embassy in Ireland.

If you’ve already read Europe Activity Log #1, you’ll know,

about a month ago I joined a Japan–Ireland Chamber of Commerce event,

connected with embassy staff there,

and they introduced me to PR staff,

so I went to the embassy and spoke with them.

As a result, they asked me,

“Would you like to provide masu for today’s event?”

and that’s how we got here.

I had no connections at the embassy to begin with,

but over these four months I’ve met many people,

received a few opportunities,

steadily grabbed them,

and somehow made it to a Japanese Embassy event.

Normally this might feel like a goal,

but for me, it’s just the starting line,

so today was a super important day!

So,

at 17:30, I headed to the Ambassador’s Residence.

They’re holding the event at the ambassador’s home?!

That surprised me too.

Of course, it’s huge!

I wasn’t a guest — I was providing masu —

so I arrived a bit early

to set them up.

I set it up like this.

I’ve done sake events many times in Japan with FOMUS,

so I’m used to this

and there was no problem.

First was the event’s main program,

an Irish talk show.

About one hour.

To be honest, my English isn’t great,

so I ignored topics I wasn’t interested in.

I only listened seriously to the crypto and AI parts. lol

🔻Event start!

After the talk show,

my real work began!

Promoting the masu.

The embassy prepared the sake,

so I chose a sake that fits masu.

We gave guests two choices —

masu or glass — but I pushed them

toward drinking with masu.

A lot of Irish and Japanese guests

enjoyed the experience of drinking from masu.

Some people even considered buying,

and I got to talk with the ambassador too.

I used the chance to serve & sample sake,

which was a nice perk. lol

I also spoke with the chairperson on the Irish side,

and they said they want me again at the end of the year.

I was really happy to hear that,

and for expanding the FOMUS brand,

it’s super effective to participate in places like this.

After the event ended and most people had left,

I drank sake and red wine with the ambassador and embassy staff.

The ambassador likes alcohol,

and I can drink too,

and they said the event was more fun because of me,

so they brought out extra red wine.

It was incredibly delicious!

There was dinner too,

but we were so into the conversation

that I had zero chance to eat,

and only edamame was left,

so we all devoured it.

In the end, we drank and chatted as a small group

until after 9 p.m.

Most people worked for the Ministry of Foreign Affairs,

and one person came from the police,

people I would never have met

in normal life.

But by being invited to this event,

I met new people.

Of course, when I was in Japan,

I had zero connections in Ireland,

so all the moving I did over these months was worth it.

In the end, you move a lot,

meet people,

they introduce you to more people,

and you keep raising your value

in a situation like this.

I’m still in a growth phase,

and I believe a brand can’t grow bigger than its founder.

That’s why

I have to get stronger,

and the people I meet and

the work partners I find will change too.

Get stronger.

I think that’s the only path to the next step.

🔻Summary

I’m deeply grateful to be invited to an event like this,

and I was able to grab this chance only because

I spent time and money taking challenges and meeting people.

For this event too, I joined one event, met someone,

they introduced me, I joined the next, met someone,

and they introduced me again...

That chain led all the way to the embassy,

and on top of that, I even received approval from

the ambassador of Ireland.

He even told me,

“You can use my name when you pitch the masu.”

I’m truly grateful for that,

and from here I’ll approach Irish companies

with FOMUS.

I was going to pitch with nothing,

but having the ambassador’s approval

is a strong appeal point,

so I want to use it well.

Also, we’re moving forward with a fully gift-focused brand for FOMUS called “MASUKAME.”

This is not the FOMUS logo —

it’s aiming for people to buy because the design looks cute at a glance.

So I’ll team up with a top-tier illustrator.

There’s a lot to do,

but I’ll face each task one by one

and expand FOMUS to the world!!

title_en: A Dublin Event for the First Time in a While
excerpt_en: It was Saturday, so I hosted my first Dublin event in two weeks. I couldn’t run it while I was on the Mediterranean trip. We had 40 reservations, but with wind and rain I wasn’t sure anyone would come. Still, six people showed up, and with a small group we could talk deeply. We shortened the rotation to 8 minutes and managed to run it once the weather eased. After the walk we chatted at a cafe, then I stayed for my card game event. I run two events: a walking event and an international exchange card game event using our original game “SILVA,” made with FOMUS members in 2023. Six people played and it got intense. I realized our GPT rulebook was easier than the website, so I may need to print clearer rules. Afterward I joined a free international coffee meetup at the same cafe, and later went to a farewell party for a girl returning to Japan. Four events in one day — my own events are still the most fun. I’ll keep running weekend events and approach companies on weekdays.
body_en:
It’s Saturday today,

so I hosted a Dublin event for the first time in two weeks!

I couldn’t run it because I was away on the Mediterranean expedition

and wasn’t in Dublin.

We had 40 reservations today,

but the wind and rain were so bad that I wondered

whether anyone would actually show up.

Still, six people came.

With fewer people, we can create time to talk with everyone,

so in a way it became a higher-satisfaction event.

Normally we do a 10-minute × 6 rotation,

but today we did 8 minutes × 6,

so we finished in about 50 minutes.

The rain and wind eased while we were walking,

so we managed to hold it safely.

After the walk,

we all gathered at a cafe to chat.

The information exchange there was really interesting,

and we decided to go out again tomorrow with the Japanese members.

Everyone has different goals,

so it’s pretty fun.

Then I stayed behind

for the card game event starting in the afternoon.

🔻SILVA is slowly growing

I run two events:

a walking event,

and an international exchange card game event.

This card game is “SILVA,”

an original game created with FOMUS members in 2023.

I used to play it a lot with Japanese members,

but now I use it as a card game event

under the banner of international exchange.

It creates international交流,

people enjoy FOMUS’s SILVA,

and they also learn about masu,

so it’s killing two birds with one stone.

Six people gathered,

and it was a very intense game!

Seeing a card game we created

played by people overseas like this

is really rewarding,

and it strengthens the connection.

We played about five rounds to confirm the rules,

and then split into two teams.

What I realized this time was

the effects of each SILVA card are a bit complex,

so I wanted people to look at the website,

but the GPT rulebook I made in advance (which covers all rules)

was apparently easier to understand, so everyone used that.

It might have been better to tell people in advance

to check the rules on the website,

or I could print out the GPT summaries

of each card’s effect beforehand.

By repeating the event,

I notice these small details,

and I can improve the experience,

so I’ll keep updating it.

🔻An event after the event

After finishing SILVA at 15:00,

another group came into the cafe.

I didn’t know until then, but

there was an international exchange event from 15:00.

So I joined that event too! lol

It was an event with zero Japanese people,

which was interesting in its own way.

It was a simple event — coffee and交流 —

but about 30 people gathered.

As expected,

free events are strong!

But for my events,

I properly charge everyone

5 euros to participate.

When you run a project,

you can’t keep it going as volunteer work,

so I take money properly and use it for operating costs.

I enjoyed this event for about 90 minutes,

but I got too hungry and left! lol

🔻Fourth event of the day

Then at night, I went to a farewell party

for a girl I met in Dublin who was returning to Japan

after an 8-month stay.

I try to join any event I’m invited to, if possible.

I stayed there for about two hours and then left.

I had plans tomorrow too,

and I had been talking nonstop since the morning,

so I was honestly tired for the first time in a while. lol

So,

after coming back to Dublin,

I ended up at four events in one day. lol

Well, I’m praising myself a bit, but

the events I host are the most fun.

Anyway,

I’ll keep running events on weekends,

and on weekdays I’ll keep approaching companies and business owners!!

excerpt_en: It was Sunday, and I intentionally left the day open in case something came up with embassy contacts. Nothing did, but a Japanese participant from yesterday’s walking event introduced a great mushroom place, so the walking members went to eat. The Australian owner spoke some Japanese, and I tried a mushroom sandwich. Later I joined the weekly Japanese meetup and unexpectedly met a guy I had advised online in Malta. I also talked with a Waseda student. I like being the listener, but when someone jumps in and monopolizes the conversation, it kills the flow. Conversations are all about back-and-forth, and I realized I need the skill to change topics when someone keeps monologuing.
body_en:
It’s Sunday today,

but I left the day open without scheduling my own event.

The reason was I thought something might come up

with embassy contacts, so I kept Sunday clear.

But there was no one to meet on Sunday. lol

Still, a Japanese person who came to yesterday’s walking event

told me about a delicious mushroom restaurant,

so the walking members went to eat there.

They sell mushrooms and donuts,

the owner is Australian,

and he could speak a little Japanese.

This time I ordered the mushroom sandwich!

It’s not exactly beautiful to look at,

but it tasted really good.

Since it’s a food truck, I wondered if I could

do some kind of collaboration with FOMUS masu,

but I couldn’t think of anything this time...

Someday it would be awesome to collaborate on something.

🔻To a Japanese meetup

From Sunday noon,

I went to the weekly Japanese meetup.

If I have time, I try to show up.

And I had an unexpected encounter there.

A guy I’d advised online about Ireland

when I was in Malta showed up.

When you keep moving, things happen.

I also talked with a Waseda University student I had just met.

There aren’t many people around my age,

and if I were living a normal life I wouldn’t be in Ireland,

and I’m not here on a typical working holiday either,

so I’m a little different from most people.

That’s why, for college students,

someone living like me is interesting.

Also, I don’t like talking nonstop,

so I usually ask questions and listen.

That back-and-forth feels very comfortable.

Then an Irish older guy jumped in

and just kept bragging about himself,

and it reminded me that

when someone talks nonstop without considering others,

conversation becomes boring.

In the end, conversation is all about back-and-forth.

When someone keeps talking about only what they want

in their own turn, it’s just self-indulgent monologuing,

and I don’t have the patience to go along with it.

I felt I need the ability to

switch topics quickly in situations like that.

title_en: Struggling with Topics I Don’t Care About
excerpt_en: In the morning I had a FOMUS meeting, and in the afternoon I studied whiskey to prepare for an Irish whiskey collaboration. I reviewed what whiskey is, its history, and the characteristics of Irish whiskey. For now, big collaborations are hard, so I’m thinking of events where we do whiskey tastings with masu, and tomorrow I’ll go to the Whiskey Museum in Dublin. I also reflected on a personal struggle: in Ireland there are few people who want to talk about AI, NFTs, or business, and I often have to listen to topics I’m not interested in. I went to a Japanese couple’s home and spent four hours mostly listening about cats and romance, trying to find value but not getting much. It made me realize how important conversational balance is and how hard it can be to stay engaged when the topics don’t connect.
body_en:
This morning,

I had a FOMUS-related meeting,

and in the afternoon, I studied whiskey.

Starting tomorrow I’m going to seriously move

toward making a collaboration with Irish whiskey happen,

so I went back and learned again:

what is whiskey?

what is the history of whiskey?

Everyone, ****

Do you know the basics of whiskey?

What is whiskey?

  • A type of distilled spirit: Alcohol fermented and distilled from grains (barley, rye, corn, etc.).
  • Aged in barrels: After distillation, it’s aged in wooden barrels (mainly oak), which deepens aroma and flavor.

Main whiskey types

  • Scotch whisky (Scotland)
  • Smoky and complex; peat is often used.
  • Irish whiskey (Ireland)
  • Smooth and easy to drink; peat is generally not used.
  • American whiskey
  • Bourbon (corn-based, sweeter) and rye whiskey (rye-based, spicier).
  • Japanese whiskey
  • Based on Scotch techniques with a uniquely delicate Japanese character.

2. Characteristics of Irish whiskey

  1. Triple distillation
  • Most whiskies are distilled twice, but Irish whiskey is distilled three times, making it smoother and cleaner.
  1. Ingredients
  • Often uses barley (malt) and unmalted barley (green malt).
  • Because peat isn’t used, the smokiness is subdued.
  1. Aging period
  • By law, it must be aged for at least three years.
  • Ireland’s mild climate results in a smooth, mellow flavor.
  1. Representative brands
  • Jameson, Bushmills, Tullamore D.E.W., Teeling.

That’s the gist.

🔻How to attack it?

Right now it’s hard to spend big money on a collaboration,

so what I can do is events like whiskey tastings using masu.

Someday, with a FOMUS brand collaboration,

we might even create a whiskey with an original logo.

*I don’t have that power yet, so it’s just in the back of my mind.

I can’t talk about whiskey without knowing it,

so tomorrow I’ll go to

the Whiskey Museum in Dublin.

I’ll learn more about Irish whiskey history

and do tastings too,

and I want to deepen my whiskey knowledge.

About 25 euros.

That’s about 4,000 yen.

I want to get learning that’s worth more than the price.

🔻I don’t know how to do small talk

One thing I’ve been struggling with lately is that

there are almost no people in Ireland

who can talk about topics I’m interested in.

I prefer talking to people who are taking on challenges

rather than people who are stable,

and I like conversations about AI, NFTs, and business.

But since I got here,

I haven’t really met people who match those topics perfectly.

There are probably just fewer people running their own businesses.

Today, a friend invited me

to visit a Japanese couple’s home.

I didn’t want to push my own stories,

and since it was a first meeting,

I decided to focus on listening.

And with five people,

if two are talkative,

a few end up listening.

The topics were things like

cats and romance,

and even for topics I wasn’t interested in,

I tried to find something to care about,

but I didn’t get much from it.

It’s not that I absolutely need to talk about AI or NFTs,

and those are so specialized that for most people

they’re confusing and boring.

Business talk isn’t interesting to most people either.

In Japan I had people around me

who could talk about that,

but in Ireland it’s difficult.

This time it was about four hours

of dinner and talk,

but I only talked about 10% of the time.

My stories are too unusual,

and I wasn’t the main character tonight,

so I felt it wasn’t good to talk too much.

In some ways, if I’m with college students or people my age,

I can match the conversation,

and they can also match me,

but lately, when the topic isn’t business

and doesn’t create any value,

I suddenly lose interest...

But most people aren’t that interested in

challenge and business talk,

so those conversations don’t happen.

And if I take the listener role,

usually I’ll switch on at some point,

but talkative people never stop,

so I stay the listener, and even if I try to speak,

I get cut off right away. lol

I’m not angry about it,

but I see it as a negative example for myself,

so I don’t become that way elsewhere.

At my own events,

I listen to the other person.

That back-and-forth helps us understand each other,

and without that it’s hard.

Honestly,

talking with about three people feels just right. lol

I’m not exactly worried about relationships,

but in this environment,

there are few business owners,

so I wonder if this is really the right environment for me.

In Japan I was around unusual people,

so I could work freely,

and I realized there are times when it’s better not to group up too much.

Of course, everyone is a good person,

and if I talked one-on-one or in a group of three,

it would be really fun.

But I realized that even if I try to be interested

in topics I don’t care about,

if I truly don’t care, it doesn’t work. lol

I’m always calmly watching who’s talking what percentage,

so if I were the one running the room,

I think I could be a bit more considerate.

I’ll write down this pathetic side of me

struggling over small things once in a while.

excerpt_en: I finally took time to approach the whiskey industry and visited the Whiskey Museum. I chose the premium plan with four tastings and a glass. The guide explained history and culture across several rooms, but her English was very fast, so I only caught about half. The tasting was the highlight, and I even brought my own masu to compare aroma and flavor. The square rim changed how the aroma hit, and there was a scent you can’t get from a glass. Staff found it interesting and told me to email for further contact. A collaboration isn’t guaranteed, but if it works it could connect to many whiskey brands and future gift sets. With Christmas season starting, I’ve been studying gift demand and store layouts. I also realized I want more non-business conversations, so I invited a same-age Irish friend to the oldest pub in Dublin and just enjoyed beer and fish and chips.
body_en:
Is there something interesting I can do with the whiskey industry?

With that in mind,

I finally went to a whiskey-related company!

I hadn’t spent much time on whiskey until now

because I couldn’t secure a big chunk of time,

so I kept putting it off.

But now I have more time,

and I’ve gotten the ambassador’s approval,

so compared to when I first arrived in Ireland,

I’ve experienced more of the culture and customs,

and this feels like the right timing to start.

So first I have to learn whiskey culture!

So,

I went to the “Whiskey Museum.”

This is a whiskey museum where you can experience and taste.

Normally it’s 20€,

but I chose the premium plan

with four tastings and a glass set.

*Normally it’s three tastings.

At first, the guide explained

the history and culture of whiskey using a video.

Then we moved rooms

and got another explanation.

Then moved again

and got another explanation.

They move by time slots,

probably to prevent crowding.

To be honest, the guide’s English was super fast

and I couldn’t even catch half. lol

It was just frustrating...

Then finally, the tasting area!

There were small pours of four different whiskeys,

and we got a lesson on enjoying the aroma and swirling,

then tasted them.

The aromas were totally different.

The taste, to me, felt not that different.

Of course, part of my purpose was to check

how it tastes when you drink from a FOMUS masu.

So of course, I brought a masu!

I checked whether the taste or aroma changed.

The rim that touches your mouth is square,

so the way aromas enter your nose is different,

and there were aromas you can’t get from a glass.

I don’t know if locals will like it,

but it didn’t feel like a total no.

When I was tasting with the masu,

a staff member noticed and I explained it,

and they were very intrigued.

“If anything, email us.

I think someone higher up will respond.”

That’s what they told me.

The person I talked to was just a staff member,

so they couldn’t really talk business,

but it was good that they said that.

I don’t know yet if a collaboration is possible,

but if I can do something with the Whiskey Museum,

I could connect with various whiskey brands too,

so in a way it feels strong,

and I thought it would be great to create gift sets with masu next year.

It feels like there are a lot of possibilities here.

I’ll also visit other distilleries soon.

🔻Gift demand

With Christmas coming up,

Dublin is in full Christmas mode.

The holiday shopping season has started.

So this is the biggest time for gift demand,

and shops are very well stocked.

At this timing,

I’ve been going around stores recently.

What designs, layouts,

and products are in demand?

That’s what I’m investigating.

As expected, gift-friendly items like

glasses, tea or coffee, or sets are very strong.

I want to add masu to that mix. lol

🔻Do we need to talk about things other than work sometimes?

Lately — actually ever since I left Japan —

I’ve been here for business, so over 90% of my conversations are business.

If I think about it, even in Japan it was 80% business,

20% anime and manga.

And even that 20% is kind of business too,

because I want to create anime,

but it’s also a hobby, so I count it as 20%. lol

One downside is

I haven’t found many people here I really click with. lol

It’s not that everyone is boring (sometimes they are),

but it’s hard for me to get into their topics.

Lately I’ve been interacting more with people

who have lived in Ireland for many years,

and then the conversation becomes about people I don’t know.

That’s just because we’ve lived in different times,

so it’s natural I don’t know, but still.

I realized there’s almost nothing I enjoy talking about

outside of business without worrying.

Basically the only theme I like is digging into the person I’m talking with.

*People who keep talking without being asked are an exception.

So,

sometimes I want to talk with someone

just for fun, without business.

(A late realization that I want that kind of time.)

That feeling popped up,

so I invited a girl my age I became friends with in Ireland

and went to an Irish pub.

We toasted at what’s apparently the oldest pub in Dublin.

“Let’s grab one drink!”

That was the plan, so we met in the evening,

but the conversation got lively,

and it turned into night, and we had dinner at the same place.

I just enjoyed delicious beer

and fish and chips.

Without any business attached,

I remembered what it felt like

to just hang out with friends the same age,

and it was simply a great time.

I don’t really complain,

and I’m better at listening than talking,

so it felt like a good day

to realize that having something like this once a month would be nice!

excerpt_en: It was Wednesday, so I went to the Japanese meetup. I’ve been joining whenever I have time since my first week in Dublin, because I can meet both new people and friends. This time I met a college student working with AI, which was rare outside of engineers in Ireland, and it was super interesting. I also met long-term residents who share info about events, and a working-holiday visitor who might place masu in Miyakojima later. That’s the positive side. But I also met a kimono-clad Japanese woman who seemed to brag and put me down. It surprised me, but it made me reflect on my own attitude and how to avoid making others uncomfortable. I don’t dislike her, and I’d talk again. The lesson is to not confront head-on but to get into their circle. Every time, the meetup brings new encounters and helps build my communication skills.
body_en:
Since it was Wednesday,

I went to the Japanese meetup at night.

This meetup is where you can meet new people

and also friends you already know,

and I’ve been joining whenever I have time

since my first week in Dublin.

This time I met a college student who uses AI for work,

and we exchanged ideas — I learned a lot myself.

People using AI are increasing,

but in Ireland I hadn’t heard of many people using it

outside of engineers,

so it was a super interesting time.

I also exchanged info with the regulars

who’ve been in Ireland a long time.

They get event information through companies I don’t know,

and they share it with me.

Then I pay to join those events! lol

*It’s worth paying to attend events.

*You never know where you’ll meet someone.

I also met someone new who came on a working holiday.

I’m on a working holiday too, so we had a lot to talk about,

and she said she’ll go to Miyakojima after this,

so we even talked about placing masu there!

Encounters like that are fun.

If I write only that, you might think “That’s just awesome,”

so I’ll share a different kind of story too.

*This isn’t a complaint — I’m just wondering why some people come across this way.

Around 10 p.m. when people were starting to leave,

I was about to go home too,

and then a Japanese woman in a kimono showed up.

She looked to be in her late 30s to 40s,

and since she didn’t know anyone,

she came to the seat where I was sitting.

The person I had been talking with was getting ready to leave,

so it was like a handoff.

For me, new connections are important,

so I decided to talk with her.

She said she works here in a nurse-like role.

She lived in London for a long time and now is in Dublin.

And she travels when she has time.

Just hearing that,

it’s pretty normal in Ireland.

But she seemed extremely proud or wanting to brag,

because in her words and attitude

there was a strong “I’m amazing, right?” vibe. lol

I hadn’t met many people who made me feel that way before,

so it surprised me.

It was in the way she spoke too,

and she would slightly put me down while talking,

then say “but I’m like this!” as if to assert herself. lol

I did wonder why a stranger was looking down on me,

but at the same time,

it made me reflect on whether I ever act that way.

Right now I haven’t accomplished anything yet,

so I have no reason to get cocky,

and I don’t act like that,

but it became a great negative example

to remind me not to speak in ways that naturally make others uncomfortable.

I don’t dislike her,

and I’d talk again if we meet,

and I did learn that she has a wide network in Ireland,

so with people like this,

it’s probably better not to confront head-on,

but to get into their circle instead.

So anyway,

there are all kinds of encounters every time,

and it’s a meetup that keeps improving my communication skills!

*I wrapped it up nicely, didn’t I!

excerpt_en: 11/28. I went to a year-end party event hosted by the Ireland–Japan Chamber of Commerce. I try to attend these because I need more connections in Ireland, and with limited budget I have no choice but to show up and expand my network. There are few Japanese-owned restaurants in Dublin, so I can’t wait for Japanese owners; I need to seriously sell masu to build FOMUS revenue. At this event I met someone from Kikkoman’s overseas sales team and learned they might help with bulk sales. It was a meaningful gathering with both new encounters and deeper talks with existing connections. The venue was Yamamori, Dublin’s largest long-standing Japanese restaurant, and I hope to place masu there eventually.
body_en:
11/28

I went to a year-end party event hosted by the Ireland–Japan Chamber of Commerce.

I attend events like this as much as possible

because I think there will be some kind of encounter.

What I’m lacking right now is connections in Ireland.

I’ve only been here four months,

and with my limited budget,

the only way I can do grassroots sales

is to go out and increase交流.

Also, in Dublin,

there are hardly any Japanese-owned Japanese restaurants,

so if I want to sell masu, I can’t insist on owners being Japanese.

I need to generate FOMUS sales,

so it’s time to seriously start pitching masu.

Originally, the plan was to provide masu

to people who resonated with FOMUS,

but if the goal is to spread masu across Europe,

FOMUS itself becomes the second phase.

First, it’s more important to make masu known to as many people as possible

and build a foundation that allows FOMUS to continue.

So, in short,

I need to start selling masu — just like with whiskey.

At this timing,

I met someone from Kikkoman’s overseas sales team

and got to hear a lot of useful things.

They said bulk sales of masu could be possible,

so I want to prepare soon

and go propose again.

So, in this way,

there were new encounters,

and I also had chances to talk more deeply

with people I already knew.

It was a very meaningful gathering.

The venue was Yamamori,

a Japanese restaurant that’s the biggest and longest-running in Ireland.

If I can place masu here,

it would be incredibly strong,

and since I have nothing to lose,

I want to prepare so I can supply masu.

So, that was another step forward

in my activities in Ireland!

title_en: What Happened When I Didn’t Think About Nutrition
excerpt_en: This is a short entry because there was little input — I got sick. With consecutive drinking parties and events plus Ireland’s climate, my body was exhausted, and my diet was messy and low in nutrition. While boiling pasta, I got dizzy and fainted with my chair, hitting my head and getting a bump. Later that night I fainted again in the kitchen; a housemate found me and helped. When you’re unwell, nothing goes right. Your body is your capital, and when you’re sick, motivation disappears, so I was reminded how important health management is.
body_en:
This one is short today.

The reason is that there wasn’t much input.

So yeah, I got sick.

Between back-to-back drinking parties and events,

and Ireland’s climate,

my body was probably exhausted.

My diet was also a mess,

and I wasn’t getting proper nutrition.

In the morning, while boiling pasta,

I got lightheaded,

sat down right away,

and fainted with the chair from anemia.

I was alone at the time,

so I just took it slow

until I recovered from the anemia.

That’s why I got a bump on my head.

I understand the feeling when I faint from anemia,

so I should have gone to a lower posture than a chair.

I also caught a cold,

so I rested in bed,

and at night I tried to go to the kitchen

to eat a light meal and take medicine,

but I got anemic again.

This time I didn’t sit on a chair,

I braced myself with my hands on the floor,

but that only lasted a moment.

I fainted.

This time my housemate was home,

noticed I had collapsed,

and took care of me.

(They gave me fish and orange juice.)

I don’t want to show this side of me, but

when you’re sick, nothing goes the way you want,

so it’s like, “it is what it is.”

Your body is your capital,

and when you feel bad, you lose motivation for everything,

so I was reminded again that health management is important.
